quotes.verbatim_quote:
Harmonizing a melody with a note six scale steps below works the same way as thirds and
tenths. A sixth below a given scale note is the same chord tone as a minor or major third above
the melody note.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Foundational theoretical statement relating sixths to thirds and tenths.

quotes.topic: Sixths as melody shadow or bass
quotes.verbatim_quote: Sixths are very useful as they can serve, like thirds, as shadows to the melody or as bass notes.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Dual functional role of sixths in arrangement.

quotes.topic: Table of Sixths key note on top rule
quotes.verbatim_quote:
By convention, scales harmonized in sixths have the key note of each scale on top, with the

harmony note a sixth below. If the key note were in the bottom position the ear would perceive the
scale as being in a minor key rather than a major key.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Governing voicing rule with acoustical reasoning.

quotes.topic: Melody on third of chord sixth below is fifth
quotes.verbatim_quote:
If the melody note is the third of the chord, as is the first note E (1) of the first measure, the note
six scale steps below is the fifth of the C chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Maps melody-chord relationship to harmonic role of sixth.

quotes.topic: Melody on root sixth below is third
quotes.verbatim_quote:
When the melody note is the root of the chord, as is the second note of the first measure, C
(2), the sixth below is the third of the Am chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Second chord-tone relationship extending analytical pattern.

quotes.topic: Melody on fifth sixth below is minor seventh
quotes.verbatim_quote:
If the melody is the fifth of the chord, as A (3) in the case of the first note of the second measure,
the sixth below will be the minor seventh interval C, of the Dm7 chord. The chord symbol above
itis a simple triad, Dm. The minor seventh interval is contained in the C scale, so it is not a clash.
You can change the C to D if you wish to keep the sound to a simple Dm chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: How diatonic sixths can imply extensions and how to adjust.

quotes.topic: Melody on dominant seventh sixth below is ninth
quotes.verbatim_quote:
When the melody note is the root of the chord, as is the second note of the first measure, C
(2), the sixth below is the third of the Am chord.

If the melody is the fifth of the chord, as A (3) in the case of the first note of the second measure,
the sixth below will be the minor seventh interval C, of the Dm7 chord. The chord symbol above
itis a simple triad, Dm. The minor
quotes.why_load_bearing: Hearing harmonic ambiguity and providing chromatic adjustment.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
As you did with thirds, take a familiar tune and harmonize it with diatonic sixths. Be careful to
use the appropriate major or minor sixth and to make adjustments. where necessary.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Practice directive linking sixths to thirds adjustment discipline.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
So here is a tune that isn’t cluttered with every arranging device, but is still totally whole,
missing nothing—one that sounds impressive but is simple to play. Yeah, | like those kind too.
There is nothing new here. The bass is slow-moving; the melodic line is mostly eighth notes. Keep
the feeling of swing going, but don’t over-exaggerate, as the tune should be played at least at a
medium tempo or faster. The tune has pairs of measures that use call-and-answer, as several of
the blues did earlier on. These sequences are what give a tune shape, and | use this compositional
style a iot.
quotes.why_load_bearing: Overarching arranging philosophy of restraint over density.
